title: S3 Presigning and CloudFront Signed Access in TypeScript · Type-System Trace: Canned Custom Policy
summary: Predict unknown input, typed state, command, stream, promise, storage, cache, security, cost, and recovery outcomes before reviewing typed signed-delivery contract; use @aws-sdk/s3-request-presigner and @aws-sdk/cloudfront-signer with exact bearer scope, key ownership, policies, expiry, and revocation limits. Planned artifact: Add and verify the canned custom policy increment in typed signed-delivery contract. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal…
objectives: In a strict Node 24 and TypeScript 7 service, choose canned or custom CloudFront policy and constrain expiry, optional start time, resource wildcard, and IP range without accidental overbreadth.
In a strict Node 24 and TypeScript 7 service, choose signed URL or signed cookie by object count, path scope, player behavior, client constraints, and leakage surface.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: S3, CloudFront, and TypeScript Predict Canned Custom Policy · Theory Canned Custom Policy
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory TypeScript file-delivery team handles case 6638 during a AWS SDK v3 dependency canary. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to bound SDK v3 signed access by exact time, resource, key, and bearer handling; do not treat TypeScript types, a resolved promise, CORS, ETag, or an emulator as stronger proof than it is; then abort during a stream or multipart promise and prove every task and part reaches a terminal state. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. Node and AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, PostgreSQL, credentials, host state, handle and stream checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Canned Custom Policy contract for the supplied data.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: In a strict Node 24 and TypeScript 7 service, choose canned or custom CloudFront policy and constrain expiry, optional start time, resource wildcard, and IP range without accidental overbreadth.

hints: For S3, CloudFront, and TypeScript Defend the Canned Custom Policy model · Theory Canned Custom Policy, name the stakeholder, unknown schema, file, object version, authorization, byte, time, promise, stream, cache, cost, abort, cleanup, and recovery boundary, Canned Custom Policy precondition, or invariant that must hold.

At S3, CloudFront, and TypeScript Defend the Canned Custom Policy model · Theory Canned Custom Policy, trace this concrete input and predict runtime validation, authorization, custody, integrity, range, stream, cache, retry, cost, cleanup, and recovery evidence before reviewing it: A theory TypeScript file-delivery team handles case 9876 during a private media entitlement change. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to bound SDK v3 signed access by exact time, resource, key, and bearer handling; keep AbortSignal and stream cleanup under one named owner; then change region, retrieval, or transfer cost and defend the revised budget action. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. Node and AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, PostgreSQL, credentials, host state, handle and stream checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: In a strict Node 24 and TypeScript 7 service, choose canned or custom CloudFront policy and constrain expiry, optional start time, resource wildcard, and IP range without accidental overbreadth.

For S3, CloudFront, and TypeScript Defend the Canned Custom Policy model · Theory Canned Custom Policy, challenge the documented misconception with runtime, file, object, policy, transfer, integrity, cache, fault, cost, restore, and controlled AWS transfer evidence: A wildcard resource in a custom policy is equivalent to signing one exact object. Type annotations, generated SDK shapes, and resolved promises do not strengthen that runtime claim.
